sections.paragraph:
Ἁρέτας, α, ὁ (on the spelling s. Dssm. NB 11 [BS 183f]) a name which, in the form H̯riṭat, is often found in Nabataean inscriptions and among the Arabs, and was borne by Nabataean kings (Joseph. index). The one named 2 Cor 11:32 was Aretas IV (ruled c. 9 b.c. to 40 a.d.). Cp. Schürer I 581–83; ASteinmann, Aretas IV: BZ 7, 1909, 174–84, 312–41; also separately 1909; Ltzm., Hdb. Exk. on 2 Cor 11:32f; HWindisch, KEK9 1924 ad loc. (lit.); JStarcky, Dict. de la Bible, Suppl. VII ’66, 913–16.—M-M.

Dssm. Dssm. = ADeissmann, various works—List 6

BS BS , s. Dssm., BS—List 6

H H = Hermas; cited, unless otherwise noted, according to MWhittaker; II a.d.—List 1

Joseph Joseph This abbr. used when no specific texts are cited (s. Jos.); I a.d.—List 5

BZ BZ = Biblische Zeitschrift—List 6

Ltzm. Ltzm. = HLietzmann, commentator and editor of various works

Hdb. Hdb. = Handbuch (in connection with a specific NT book, the ref. is to a commentary in the series Handbuch zum Neuen Testament, founded by HLietzmann)—List 6

KEK KEK = Kritisch-exegetischer Kommentar über das NT—List 6

ad loc. ad loc. = ad locum (to or at the place under consideration)

lit. lit. = literal(ly); literature (refererences to [scholarly] literature)

Suppl.

Suppl. = Supplement (to serials)

suppl. = supplement, supplied (in grammatical references)

M-M M-M = JMoulton/GMilligan, Vocabulary of Greek Testament—Lists 4, 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 130
